original_abstract:
Efforts to commercialize high-energy-density lithium metal anodes (LMAs) are often hindered by low cyclability resulting from the continuous growth of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI). While modern liquid electrolytes have achieved Coulombic efficiencies (CE) exceeding 95%, the specific mechanisms driving capacity loss through SEI formation and maturation remain poorly understood. In this work, we investigate the solubility of the SEI as a critical factor in its stability and passivating efficacy. Using in-operando electrochemical quartz crystal microbalance (EQCM), we systematically quantify the mass loss of SEIs derived from various ether-based electrolytes, including high-performance localized high-concentration electrolytes (LHCE). 

Our findings reveal a significant correlation between SEI solubility, anode passivity, and overall battery cyclability. We demonstrate that even state-of-the-art electrolytes produce interphases that undergo substantial dissolution during rest, leading to a cycle of degradation and repair that consumes active lithium. Through a combination of EQCM, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, we show that SEI solubility is governed by a complex interplay between the chemical composition of the interphase and the physical properties of the electrolyte, such as salt concentration and solvent coordination. Furthermore, we illustrate that shifting the solubility equilibrium—either through electrolyte engineering or reducing electrolyte volume—can markedly extend cycle life. This study identifies SEI dissolution as a primary pathway for capacity decay and provides new design principles for developing passivating interphases to enhance both the cycle and calendar life of lithium metal batteries.
humanized_abstract:
Lithium metal anodes with high energy density have not yet reached the commercialization stage due to low cycling stability attributed to the permanent thickening of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI). Though many liquid electrolytes have reached a Coulombic efficiency (CE) over 95%, the SEI thickening and the corresponding capacity loss mechanism remains unclear. Herein, the solubility of the SEI is probed as a key factor influencing its stability and functionality. The in-operando Electrochemical Quartz Crystal Microbalance (EQCM) is utilized to study the mass loss of SEIs formed from various ether-based electrolytes, including localized high-concentration electrolytes (LHCEs).

We show that SEI solubility, anode passivity, and lithium metal battery cyclability are strongly correlated. We observe that, regardless of electrolyte composition, new SEI is continually being dissolved and rebuilt over each cycle, which contributes to significant capacity loss. This is demonstrated using techniques such as EQCM, XPS, and 1 H and 7 Li NMR. Our results show that the solubility of the SEI film is dependent on both the chemistry of the SEI film itself as well as the electrolyte solution (electrolyte composition and physical properties such as salt concentration and solvent coordination). Finally, by understanding how to shift the solubility balance of the SEI film through changes to electrolyte solution, such as adjusting electrolyte composition or using less electrolyte, we can dramatically increase lithium metal battery cyclability. This research uncovers the contributions of SEI dissolution to lithium metal battery capacity loss, while demonstrating methods for the design of a functional SEI film that can increase the cyclability and lifespan of lithium metal batteries.

document.input:
Efforts to commercialize high-energy-density lithium metal anodes (LMAs) are often hindered by low cyclability resulting from the continuous growth of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI). While modern liquid electrolytes have achieved Coulombic efficiencies (CE) exceeding 95%, the specific mechanisms driving capacity loss through SEI formation and maturation remain poorly understood.
